population: Children or adolescents with relevant child medication allergy documentation history, findings and clinician decisions entered.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding. The following guidance is based on the best available evidence. The full guideline gives details of the methods and the evidence used to develop the guidance.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When assessing a person presenting with possible drug allergy, take a history and undertake a clinical examination. Use the following tables as a guide when deciding whether to suspect drug allergy.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Note that these tables describe common and important presenting features of drug allergy but other presentations are also recognised. Table 1 Immediate, rapidly evolving reactions Signs and allergic patterns of suspected drug allergy Timing of onset Anaphylaxis – a severe multi‑system reaction characterised by: erythema, urticaria or angioedema and hypotension and/or bronchospasm Onset usually less than 1 hour after drug exposure (previous exposure not always confirmed) Urticaria or angioedema without systemic features Onset usually less than 1 hour after drug exposure (previous exposure not always confirmed) Exacerbation of asthma (for example, with non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs [NSAIDs]) Onset usually less than 1 hour after drug exposure (previous exposure not always confirmed) Table 2 Non‑immediate reactions without systemic involvement Signs and allergic patterns of suspected

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that the reaction is more likely to be caused by drug allergy if it occurred during or after use of the drug and: the drug is known to cause that type of reaction or the person has previously had a similar reaction to that drug or drug class.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that the reaction is less likely to be caused by drug allergy if: there is a possible non‑drug cause for the person's symptoms (for example, they have had similar symptoms when not taking the drug) or the person has gastrointestinal symptoms only.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: After a suspected drug‑related anaphylactic reaction, take 2 blood samples for mast cell tryptase in line with recommendations in the NICE guideline on anaphylaxis .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Document people's drug allergy status in their medical records using 1 of the following: 'drug allergy' 'none known' 'unable to ascertain' (document it as soon as the information is available).

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If drug allergy status has been documented, record all of the following at a minimum: the drug name the signs, symptoms and severity of the reaction (see recommendation 1.1.1) the date when the reaction occurred.

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When a person presents with suspected drug allergy, document their reaction in a structured approach that includes: the generic and proprietary name of the drug or drugs suspected to have caused the reaction, including the strength and formulation a description of the reaction (see recommendation 1.1.1) the indication for the drug being taken (if there is no clinical diagnosis, describe the illness) the date and time of the reaction the number of doses taken or number of days on the drug before onset of the reaction the route of administration which drugs or drug classes to avoid in future.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Prescriptions (paper or electronic) issued in any healthcare setting should be standardised and redesigned to record information on which drugs or drug classes to avoid to reduce the risk of drug allergy.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Ensure that drug allergy status is documented separately from adverse drug reactions and that it is clearly visible to all healthcare professionals who are prescribing drugs.

evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Check a person's drug allergy status and confirm it with them (or their family members or carers as appropriate) before prescribing, dispensing or administering any drug (see also recommendation 1.3.4). Update the person's medical records or inform their GP if there is a change in drug allergy status.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Allergy specialists should give the following written information to people who have undergone specialist drug allergy investigation: the diagnosis – whether they had an allergic or non‑allergic reaction the drug name and a description of their reaction (see recommendation 1.1.1) the investigations used to confirm or exclude the diagnosis drugs or drug classes to avoid in future any safe alternative drugs that may be used.

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If drug allergy is suspected: consider stopping the drug suspected to have caused the allergic reaction and advising the person to avoid that drug in future treat the symptoms of the acute reaction if needed; send people with severe reactions to hospital document details of the suspected drug allergy in the person's medical records (see recommendations 1.2.3 and 1.2.6) provide the person with information (see section 1.3).
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If drug allergy is suspected: consider stopping the drug suspected to have caused the allergic reaction and advising the person to avoid that drug in future treat the symptoms of the acute reaction if needed; send people with severe reactions to hospital document details of the suspected drug allergy in the person's medical records (see recommendations 1.2.3 and 1.2.6) provide the person with info

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people to a specialist drug allergy service if they have had: a suspected anaphylactic reaction (also see the NICE guideline on anaphylaxis ) or a severe non‑immediate cutaneous reaction (for example, drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms [DRESS], Stevens–Johnson Syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis).
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people to a specialist drug allergy service if they have had: a suspected anaphylactic reaction (also see the NICE guideline on anaphylaxis ) or a severe non‑immediate cutaneous reaction (for example, drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms [DRESS], Stevens–Johnson Syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis).

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Explain to people with a suspected allergy to a non‑selective non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug (NSAID) (and their family members or carers as appropriate) that in future they need to avoid all non‑selective NSAIDs, including over‑the‑counter preparations.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people with a suspected allergy to a non‑selective non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug (NSAID) (and their family members or carers as appropriate) that in future they need to avoid all non‑selective NSAIDs, including over‑the‑counter preparations.

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services

evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people who have had a mild allergic reaction to a non‑selective NSAID but need an anti‑inflammatory: discuss the benefits and risks of selective cyclooxygenase 2 (COX‑2) inhibitors (including the low risk of drug allergy) consider introducing a selective COX‑2 inhibitor at the lowest starting dose with only a single dose on the first day.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not offer a selective COX‑2 inhibitor to people in a non‑specialist setting if they have had a severe reaction, such as anaphylaxis, severe angioedema or an asthmatic reaction, to a non‑selective NSAID.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Do not offer a selective COX‑2 inhibitor to people in a non‑specialist setting if they have had a severe reaction, such as anaphylaxis, severe angioedema or an asthmatic reaction, to a non‑selective NSAID.

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people who need treatment with an NSAID to a specialist drug allergy service if they have had a suspected allergic reaction to an NSAID with symptoms such as anaphylaxis, severe angioedema or an asthmatic reaction.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that people with asthma who also have nasal polyps are likely to have NSAID‑sensitive asthma unless they are known to have tolerated NSAIDs in the last 12 months.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that people with asthma who also have nasal polyps are likely to have NSAID‑sensitive asthma unless they are known to have tolerated NSAIDs in the last 12 months.

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
Non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (including selective cyclooxygenase 2 inhibitors)

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people with a suspected allergy to beta‑lactam antibiotics to a specialist drug allergy service if they: need treatment for a disease or condition that can only be treated by a beta‑lactam antibiotic or are likely to need beta‑lactam antibiotics frequently in the future (for example, people with recurrent bacterial infections or immune deficiency).
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with a suspected allergy to beta‑lactam antibiotics to a specialist drug allergy service if they: need treatment for a disease or condition that can only be treated by a beta‑lactam antibiotic or are likely to need beta‑lactam antibiotics frequently in the future (for example, people with recurrent bacterial infections or immune deficiency).

heading_path: 1.4 Non‑specialist management and referral to specialist services

evidence_statements.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider referring people to a specialist drug allergy service if they are not able to take beta‑lactam antibiotics and at least 1 other class of antibiotic because of suspected allergy to these antibiotics.
evidence_statements.source_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring people to a specialist drug allergy service if they are not able to take beta‑lactam antibiotics and at least 1 other class of antibiotic because of suspected allergy to these antibiotics.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people to a specialist drug allergy service if they need a procedure involving a local anaesthetic that they are unable to have because of suspected allergy to local anaesthetics.
